my_sentence: Today was a Saturday, so I can go some places with my family.
correction: Today was Saturday, so I went to some places with my family.
explanation: The action happened today (past), so the verb must be past tense: 'went', not 'can go'. Also 'go to places', not 'go places'.
correct_examples: Yesterday I went to a few places with my family.
It was the weekend, so we went out together.
context: Talking about the weekend

my_sentence: Even though you have a big dream, you need to start up from small.
correction: Even though you have a big dream, you need to build a start-up business.
explanation: 'Start-up' here is a noun (a new business), not a verb phrase. You can't say 'start up from small'; use it as a noun: 'build a start-up business'.
correct_examples: He built a small start-up that grew into a big company.
Many start-up businesses fail in the first year.
context: Using vocabulary 'start-up' in a sentence

my_sentence: I'm in seizure, feel the company, that need a lot of finance.
correction: That needs a lot of finances.
explanation: Third-person singular subject 'that' takes 'needs'. Also 'finances' is normally plural when it means monetary resources.
correct_examples: That project needs a lot of finances.
Running a company needs strong finances.
context: Using vocabulary 'finances' in a sentence

my_sentence: We need to make effort to finish it.
correction: We need to make an effort to finish it.
explanation: 'Make an effort' needs the article 'an' (a single, countable effort). Without it the phrase is ungrammatical.
correct_examples: You need to make an effort to improve.
She made an effort to be on time.
context: Using vocabulary 'finances' in a sentence

my_sentence: We need to squirrel away water to prevent the snowy day from the weather.
correction: ... to prevent the snowy day from happening.
explanation: The pattern is 'prevent + something + from + -ing'. After 'from' you need a gerund ('happening'), not a noun phrase.
correct_examples: We took steps to prevent the problem from happening.
Nothing could prevent the accident from happening.
context: Using vocabulary 'squirrel away' in a sentence

my_sentence: If we have enough money, we can fix the most problems that happened in our lives.
correction: ... we can fix most problems that can happen in our lives.
explanation: You are talking about problems in general (any time), so use 'can happen', not the past 'happened'. Also drop 'the' before 'most problems'.
correct_examples: Money can solve most problems that can happen in life.
We can handle most issues that can come up.
context: Whether it's important to get rich

my_sentence: There are many people, a more effort than you.
correction: There are many people who do more effort than you. / There are many people who work harder than you.
explanation: You need a relative pronoun 'who' to connect 'people' to the clause describing them. A more natural verb is 'work harder' rather than 'do more effort'.
correct_examples: There are many people who work harder than you.
There are colleagues who put in more effort than I do.
context: Whether effort alone makes you rich

my_sentence: So you can't depend it alone.
correction: So you can't depend on it alone.
explanation: 'Depend' is intransitive and needs the preposition 'on': 'depend on something'.
correct_examples: You can't depend on luck alone.
Success depends on many factors.
context: Whether effort alone makes you rich

my_sentence: We need to keep calm to control our...
correction: We need to calm ourselves down.
explanation: Use the reflexive 'calm ourselves down' to express controlling one's own emotions; 'keep calm to control' is incomplete and unnatural here.
correct_examples: When you're angry, you need to calm yourself down.
Take a breath and calm yourself down.
context: Discussing greed and self-control

my_sentence: I met some people who are really greedy.
correction: I met some people who were really greedy.
explanation: Because the main clause is past ('met'), the relative clause should backshift to past too: 'who were'.
correct_examples: I met a man who was very kind.
We talked to students who were nervous about the exam.
context: Talking about greedy people Jack has met

my_sentence: Someone can cheat you and let you write a signature to give up your everything.
correction: Someone can cheat on you ... to give up everything you own.
explanation: The teacher used 'cheat on you' here. Also 'your everything' is unnatural; say 'everything you own'.
correct_examples: They might cheat on you to take your property.
He was tricked into giving up everything he owned.
context: Disadvantages of being rich and old

my_sentence: They will envy it about you.
correction: They envy you.
explanation: 'Envy' is transitive and takes a direct object with no preposition: 'envy you', not 'envy it about you'.
correct_examples: They envy you for your success.
Some classmates envied her good grades.
context: Being envied by others

my_sentence: Our level become bigger and bigger.
correction: Our gap becomes bigger and bigger.
explanation: Singular subject takes 'becomes' (third-person -s). Jack repeatedly drops the -s on present-tense verbs.
correct_examples: The gap between us becomes wider every year.
His income becomes higher each year.
context: Why people envy others

my_sentence: I will envy you and try to let you back.
correction: I will envy you and try to push you down.
explanation: 'Let you back' doesn't convey the intended meaning. The natural idiom for holding someone back out of envy is 'push you down'.
correct_examples: Jealous people may try to push you down.
Don't let others push you down.
context: Why people envy others

my_sentence: You do not have the ability to make money, you get it just luck.
correction: You just got lucky.
explanation: Natural English uses the adjective 'lucky' with 'got': 'you got lucky'. Past tense because it already happened. 'Get it just luck' is not idiomatic.
correct_examples: He didn't work for it, he just got lucky.
We got lucky with the weather.
context: Gossip an envious person spreads

my_sentence: If your salary higher than your friend, your friends will be angry.
correction: If your salary is higher than your friend's, ...
explanation: A clause needs a verb: insert the linking verb 'is'. Also use the possessive 'your friend's' (salary) for the comparison.
correct_examples: If your salary is higher than your friend's, they may feel jealous.
If the rent is higher than last year's, we'll move.
context: Comparing salaries among friends

my_sentence: If a celebrity want to keep his reputation, he has to do many things.
correction: If a celebrity wants to keep his reputation, ...
explanation: Singular subject 'a celebrity' takes 'wants' (third-person -s).
correct_examples: If a student wants to pass, he has to study.
If a company wants to grow, it needs investment.
context: How fans control a celebrity's life

my_sentence: In the past they are famous, they can earn a lot of money.
correction: In the past they could be famous and could earn a lot of money.
explanation: With 'in the past' you must use past tense: 'could be' and 'could earn', not the present 'are' and 'can'.
correct_examples: In the past they could travel freely.
Back then, we could buy a lot with little money.
context: What happens when idols lose their fans

my_sentence: They should be more carefully about their behaviors.
correction: They should be more careful about their behavior.
explanation: After the linking verb 'be' you need an adjective ('careful'), not an adverb ('carefully'). 'Behavior' is usually uncountable.
correct_examples: You should be more careful with your money.
She is careful about what she says.
context: Idols needing to avoid scandals

my_sentence: Saving cash at home is not secure than putting it in the bank.
correction: It is safer to save money in a bank. / Keeping cash at home is not as secure as putting it in a bank.
explanation: 'Not secure than' is ungrammatical. Use 'not as secure as' for an equal comparison, or rephrase with the comparative 'safer'.
correct_examples: Cash at home is not as safe as money in a bank.
It is safer to keep your savings in a bank.
context: Advantages of saving money in a bank

my_sentence: If I save my money in the bank, it keeps my money safe.
correction: If I save my money in the bank, it keeps it safe.
explanation: Don't repeat the same noun ('money') within one sentence; replace the second mention with a pronoun ('it'). The teacher flagged this repeatedly today.
correct_examples: I put my money in the bank because it keeps it safe.
She bought the book and read it that night.
context: Explaining the safety of saving in a bank

my_sentence: If your card was stolen and you lose some money, the bank will pay you.
correction: If your card has been stolen and you lose some money, the bank will pay you the full amount.
explanation: Use the present perfect passive 'has been stolen' for a recent event whose result still matters now, rather than the simple past 'was stolen'.
correct_examples: If your card has been stolen, call the bank immediately.
Once the password has been changed, log in again.
context: Banks compensating for theft

my_sentence: If you store the money in the bank, even though it go to bankrupt one day, you can get your money back.
correction: ... even though it goes bankrupt one day, you can get your money back.
explanation: Singular 'it' takes 'goes'. The idiom is 'go bankrupt' (no 'to'): 'goes bankrupt'.
correct_examples: Even if the bank goes bankrupt, your savings are protected.
If the company goes bankrupt, employees lose their jobs.
context: Safety of money in a bank

my_sentence: If you save your money in the bank, you can easily to access them.
correction: If you save your money in the bank, you can access it easily.
explanation: After a modal ('can') use the bare verb 'access', not 'to access' (an infinitive can't be the main verb). Put the adverb 'easily' after the object. Also use 'it' for the uncountable 'money'.
correct_examples: You can access your account easily online.
I can finish it quickly.
context: Advantage: easy access to bank savings

my_sentence: You will very care about stock prices.
correction: You will care more about stock prices.
explanation: 'Very' can't modify the verb 'care'. Use 'care more about' (or 'care a lot about').
correct_examples: If you invest, you'll care more about the news.
He cares a lot about his grades.
context: Why investors watch stock prices

my_sentence: They will be affected, sometimes they become angry and sometimes they become happy.
correction: They can be affected; sometimes they are angry and at other times they are happy.
explanation: Use the modal 'can' for possibility rather than 'will'. With 'sometimes' describing a state, 'they are angry/happy' is more natural than 'become'.
correct_examples: Prices can be affected by the news.
Sometimes he is happy, at other times he is tired.
context: How coworkers react to stock prices

my_sentence: They can use their parents' money to expense.
correction: They can use the money in many ways.
explanation: 'Expense' is not used as a verb meaning 'to spend'. Rephrase, e.g. 'use the money in many ways' or 'spend the money on ...'.
correct_examples: They can use the money in many ways.
She spent the money on education and a house.
context: How children might use parents' savings
